title: The Damage a Word Does When It Lands

paragraphs: Every word for an emotion was once coined in the absence of a better one. The coinage was not neutral. It selected from the full spectrum of what was felt and handed the rest over to silence.
To name something is to make it manageable, but management is not the same as understanding. The named thing shrinks slightly to fit inside the word.
Languages that lack a single word for a particular state are not deficient. They are forced to describe rather than label. The description is harder but more honest about complexity.
Translation is not the movement of a meaning from one house into another. It is the construction of a new house from materials that belonged to a different climate.

There is something mourned in every accurate translation and something quietly invented that was never in the original. The gap between the two is not an error. It is where languages touch without meeting.
essay.blockquote: The word is a door that closes on the thing it names.
essay.crosslink: In information theory, compression always involves loss — and translation is a form of lossy compression applied to lived experience rather than to binary data.

number.label: The approximate number of living languages currently documented — each one a different architecture for sorting human experience into speakable units.

fragment: He tried to explain the feeling to her in three languages. In the first, the word was too clinical. In the second, the word was too religious. In the third, there was no word, only a phrase that meant something like: the sound a door makes when a room has been empty for a long time. She said: yes, that one. He did not know which language she meant.

word.origin: Arabic — a threshold, interval, or barrier between two states; in Islamic cosmology, the intermediate realm between life and the afterlife, though the word's application extends to any liminal in-between.
word.definition: An interval or threshold that separates two conditions without fully belonging to either.

quote.text: Las palabras son símbolos que postulan una memoria compartida.
quote.translation: Words are symbols that postulate a shared memory.
quote.author: Jorge Luis Borges
quote.source: Otras inquisiciones, 'El idioma analítico de John Wilkins'

person.name: Victor Segalen
paragraphs: Victor Segalen was a French naval physician and writer who developed a theory of exoticism not as the consumption of the foreign but as the irreducible distance between the perceiver and the perceived. He argued that the exotic was defined by its resistance to being fully known, and that any attempt to absorb the other — aesthetically, linguistically, culturally — was a form of violence against that resistance.

He wrote in Chinese, studied Polynesian funeral chants, and died in a forest in Brittany at forty-one, possibly from a fall. His major theoretical work, Essay on Exoticism, was never finished. What survives is a notebook of fragments, each one pressing against a limit it declines to cross.

questions.text: Which emotion do you most often name with a word that does not quite fit?
questions.deeper: What does the wrong word make easier, and what does it make invisible?
questions.experiment: Describe one feeling tonight using only physical sensations and locations in the body, without naming the emotion.

questions.text: What would you lose if every language had a word for every state you could experience?
questions.deeper: Is something preserved by the silence around un-named things?
questions.experiment: Invent a word tonight for a state you experience regularly that has no name in any language you know.

questions.text: When you translate yourself to someone else, what is the part that arrives intact and what is the part that was lost in transit?
questions.deeper: Is communication ever more than approximate?
questions.experiment: Tell someone something tonight by describing rather than naming it.

trivia.question: What is the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis primarily concerned with?
options: The rate at which languages borrow vocabulary from each other
The influence of language structure on thought and perception
The genetic relationship between Indo-European languages
The minimum vocabulary required for functional communication
trivia.explanation: The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis, in its strong form, argues that language determines thought; in its weaker and more widely accepted form, it proposes that language influences how speakers perceive and categorise experience.

data.before: Translation is not the movement of a meaning from one house to another — it is the construction of a new house from materials that belonged to a different
data.hint: The essay uses this word to describe what makes the original materials belong somewhere else.
